description: Zones and conduits are the fundamental organizing principle of IEC 62443 security architecture. A Zone is a logical grouping of assets with equivalent security level requirements. A Conduit is a communication path connecting zones — the security policy at each conduit controls what data can flow between the zones it connects.

zoneDefinition.definition: A logical or physical grouping of assets (physical, logical, cyber, human) that share common security requirements based on criticality, consequence of compromise, and operational function. All assets within a zone are subject to the same security level target (SL-T) and the same set of security policies.
zoningPrinciples: Assets with the same criticality and function belong in the same zone
Assets that need to communicate frequently should be in the same zone to minimize conduit overhead
Safety systems (SIS) must be in their own isolated zone — never shared with process control systems
Engineering workstations and operator HMIs should be in separate zones (different trust levels)
Vendor remote access should terminate in a dedicated zone (jump zone) — never directly in control zones
Zone boundaries should align with network segments where possible (VLAN, physical separation)

conduitDefinition.definition: A logical grouping of communication channels between two or more zones that shares common security characteristics. Conduits are the data pathways that cross zone boundaries — every inter-zone communication flows through a conduit. The security controls applied to a conduit determine what can flow between zones and how it is protected.
conduitPrinciples: All inter-zone communications must pass through a defined conduit
Conduits must enforce the security policy of the lower of the two zone SL targets
Deny-all with explicit permit is the required conduit policy
Conduits must be documented with permitted protocols, source/destination addresses, and business justification
High-risk conduits (IT-to-OT) should use unidirectional gateways or data diodes

referenceZones.name: Safety Instrumented System Zone
referenceZones.description: Contains all Safety Instrumented System controllers and related hardware. Must be physically and network-isolated from all other zones. No incoming connections permitted — data flows outward only via data diode or unidirectional gateway to monitoring systems.
keyControls: Physical air gap from DCS network
Data diode for outbound safety data to monitoring
Physical access control (locked cabinet, personnel logging)
No remote access under any circumstances
Separate engineering network physically isolated
referenceZones.nacsaRelevance: SIS protecting designated NCII processes are the highest-criticality assets within scope of NACSA Act 854 s18.

referenceZones.name: Distributed Control System / Basic Control Zone
referenceZones.description: Contains primary process controllers (DCS, PLCs) for critical process control functions. Communicates with Level 2 supervisory zone via controlled conduit. No internet connectivity. No IT network connectivity.
typicalAssets: Field junction boxes with network connectivity
keyControls: No direct connection to IT network
Firewall at conduit to HMI zone
Change management for all controller modifications
Firmware integrity verification
Physical access control
referenceZones.nacsaRelevance: DCS zone assets are core NCII assets for energy, water, and manufacturing sectors.

referenceZones.name: HMI and Supervisory Zone
referenceZones.description: Contains operator HMIs, SCADA servers, and operator workstations. Operators interact with the process through systems in this zone. Engineering workstations should be in a separate zone.
keyControls: Application whitelisting
No internet access
Patch management with OT-vendor approval
Network separation from engineering zone
Session lock enforcement
referenceZones.nacsaRelevance: SCADA data from HMI zone is primary evidence for NACSA s23 audits.

referenceZones.name: Engineering Workstation Zone
referenceZones.description: Contains engineering workstations used for PLC programming, DCS configuration, and HMI development. Requires elevated SL because access to this zone provides direct ability to modify controller logic.
typicalAssets: PLC programming workstations (STEP 7, TIA Portal, Studio 5000)
keyControls: Strict access control — engineering personnel only
Jump server required for remote access
All access sessions logged and recorded
Engineering workstations never used for email or browsing
USB restricted to approved and scanned media only
Privileged access management (PAM)
referenceZones.nacsaRelevance: Engineering workstation compromise provides direct capability to modify NCII control logic — highest-value target after SIS.

referenceZones.name: Historian / Level 3 Zone
referenceZones.description: Contains historian servers, batch management, and MES systems. This zone aggregates data from multiple Level 2 zones and provides data to the IDMZ for IT consumption.
keyControls: Separate VLAN from HMI zone
OPC-UA preferred over OPC-DA for historian connectivity
Database hardening
Access restricted to operations and IT integration services
referenceZones.nacsaRelevance: Historian data supports NACSA s21 risk assessment evidence and operational analytics.

referenceZones.name: Industrial DMZ Zone
referenceZones.description: Buffer zone between OT (Levels 0-3) and IT (Levels 4-5). All data exchange between OT and IT must pass through IDMZ services. No direct OT-to-IT connectivity permitted. IDMZ hosts controlled data exchange services.
keyControls: Deny-all at both IDMZ boundaries with explicit permits only
All traffic flows documented and reviewed quarterly
Jump server sessions logged and recorded
IDMZ systems patched and hardened
No direct IT email or internet access from IDMZ
referenceZones.nacsaRelevance: IDMZ is the primary enforcer of NACSA s18 data security measures and the key control point for supply chain and vendor access.

referenceZones.name: Remote Access Zone
referenceZones.description: Dedicated zone for all vendor and third-party remote access. Vendor sessions terminate here and are proxied to OT zones through managed jump services — vendors never get direct OT network access.
keyControls: MFA required for all remote access
Session recording — 100% of sessions
Maximum session duration with forced re-authentication
Vendor access requires per-session approval
No split tunnelling — all traffic through corporate network
referenceZones.nacsaRelevance: Remote vendor access is the most common initial access vector in OT incidents. Robust remote access zone controls directly support NACSA s18 risk mitigation.

referenceConduits.name: SIS to Monitoring (unidirectional)
prohibitedFlows: Any inbound traffic to SIS zone

referenceConduits.name: DCS to HMI
prohibitedFlows: RDP from HMI zone to DCS zone

referenceConduits.name: HMI Zone to Engineering Zone
permittedFlows: Engineering protocol traffic only (STEP 7, TIA Portal, Studio 5000 ports)

referenceConduits.name: HMI/SCADA to Historian
prohibitedFlows: RDP from historian to HMI zone

referenceConduits.name: Historian to IDMZ (data replication)
referenceConduits.mechanism: Data diode (preferred) or firewall with strict outbound-only rules
prohibitedFlows: Any inbound data modification commands from IDMZ to historian

referenceConduits.name: IDMZ to IT Network
referenceConduits.mechanism: Firewall — separate from OT-side firewall (two-firewall IDMZ design)
permittedFlows: Historian mirror data to IT analytics
Patch downloads to IDMZ patch server
Authorised remote access sessions (inbound, via jump server only)
